content.method: Not applicable as an empirical method; this requires deliberative stakeholder processes (e.g., structured public consultation, multi-criteria decision analysis with explicit value weighting) to make the scoping choice transparent rather than resolve it via evidence.
content.question: What is the true scope of the problem any reform is meant to address — the systemic 85% selectivity of Hungarian secondary schools, the narrower 9-10% enrolled in 6/8-year tracks, the disruptiveness of the age-10 transition, or underlying parental distrust of state upper-primary schools?
content.why_it_matters: This question exposes a genuine value/framing disagreement rather than an empirical gap: different definitions of 'the problem' imply entirely different proposal sets (T2/T5 target track structure; T3 targets admissions; T4 targets trust/quality). Treating this as resolvable by more data would misrepresent a normative scoping choice that policymakers must make explicitly.
